categories.name: Broken Access Control
categories.description: Access control enforces policy such that users cannot act outside of their intended permissions. Failures lead to unauthorized information disclosure, modification or destruction of data, or performing business functions outside the user's limits.

key_cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
Exposure of Sensitive Information Through Sent Data

common_vulnerabilities: Violation of least privilege or default-deny (default-allow instead)
Bypassing access control by tampering with URL, parameters, or API requests
Insecure direct object references (IDOR) — viewing or editing another user's data by modifying identifiers
Missing access control on API endpoints for POST, PUT, DELETE
Privilege escalation — acting as admin when logged in as standard user
JWT token tampering, metadata manipulation, or replay of expired tokens
CORS misconfiguration allowing unauthorized cross-origin API access
Force browsing to unauthenticated or admin-only pages
SSRF — server makes requests to attacker-controlled URLs via unvalidated user input
prevention: Implement access control server-side where the attacker cannot modify it
Default-deny: reject all access except to explicitly public resources
Centralize access control mechanisms and reuse across the application
Enforce record ownership at the model/data layer, not just the UI
Disable directory listing and remove sensitive metadata from web roots
Log and alert on access control failures
Rate-limit API and controller access to minimize automated attack damage
Invalidate sessions server-side on logout; use short-lived stateless tokens (JWT)

categories.name: Security Misconfiguration
categories.description: A system, application, or cloud service is set up incorrectly from a security perspective, creating exploitable vulnerabilities. Includes missing hardening, unnecessary features, unchanged defaults, overly verbose errors, missing security headers, and misconfigured permissions.

key_cwes.name: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference (XXE)
Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains

common_vulnerabilities: Missing security hardening across any part of the application stack
Unnecessary features enabled (ports, services, pages, accounts, privileges)
Default accounts and passwords unchanged
Error handling reveals stack traces or overly informative error messages to users
Security headers missing or set to insecure values (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
Software is out of date or has known vulnerabilities left unpatched
XXE: XML parsers process external entity references, enabling file read or SSRF
Cloud storage permissions default to public or internet-accessible
CORS wildcard or overly permissive origins configured
prevention: Implement repeatable hardening process for consistent secure deployment across dev/QA/prod
Minimal platform: remove unused features, components, documentation, and sample apps
Review and update configurations as part of the patch management process
Segment application architecture with containerization or ACLs
Send security directives to clients (security headers)
Automate verification of configurations in all environments
Use identity federation and short-lived credentials instead of hardcoded secrets
Disable XML external entity processing in all XML parsers

categories.name: Software Supply Chain Failures
categories.description: Breakdowns in the creation, distribution, or updating of software. Vulnerabilities or malicious changes in third-party code, tools, or dependencies that the system relies on. Expanded from 'Vulnerable and Outdated Components' to cover full supply chain attacks.

key_cwes.name: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
Reliance on Component That is Not Updateable

common_vulnerabilities: Using components with known CVEs (direct or transitive dependencies)
Not knowing which versions of components are in use (no SBOM)
Components unmaintained or abandoned with no security patches available
Compromised vendor update mechanisms (SolarWinds-style attacks)
Malicious packages in registries (typosquatting, dependency confusion)
Self-propagating supply chain worms (npm/PyPI)
CI/CD pipeline compromise allowing injection of malicious build steps
Installing packages from unofficial or untrusted sources without hash verification
prevention: Maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for all direct and transitive dependencies
Continuously monitor CVE/NVD/OSV databases for vulnerabilities in dependencies
Source components exclusively from official, trusted repositories with signed packages
Pin dependency versions deliberately; don't float to latest implicitly
Apply patches timely using a risk-based approach with staged/canary deployments
Enable MFA across code repositories, developer workstations, and build servers
Enforce separation of duties: no single person deploys without oversight
Implement signed builds and immutable artifacts
Use automated software composition analysis (SCA) tools
notable_incidents: SolarWinds (2019): compromised vendor update infected ~18,000 organizations
Log4Shell CVE-2021-44228: Apache Log4j RCE via JNDI lookup
Bybit (2025): supply chain attack on wallet software, $1.5B theft
Shai-Hulud (2025): first self-propagating npm worm, 500+ package versions

categories.name: Cryptographic Failures
categories.description: Failures related to cryptography that lead to exposure of sensitive data. Includes absent encryption, use of weak or deprecated algorithms, improper key management, and missing transport-layer security. Sensitive data requiring extra protection includes passwords, credit card numbers, health records, personal information, and business secrets.

key_cwes.name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
Not Using an Unpredictable IV with CBC Mode
Use of a One-Way Hash Without a Salt
Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort

common_vulnerabilities: Data transmitted in cleartext (HTTP, FTP, SMTP without TLS)
Deprecated algorithms used: MD5, SHA-1, DES, 3DES, RC4
ECB mode used for block cipher encryption
Weak or default cryptographic keys; keys committed to source control
No key rotation or keys used past expiration
Passwords stored with simple/fast hashing (SHA-256) instead of adaptive algorithms
Missing or weak salt on password hashes
Using random module (PRNG) instead of secrets/CSPRNG for security-sensitive values
TLS certificate validation disabled (verify=False)
Missing HSTS header allowing protocol downgrade attacks
Initialization vectors reused or predictable
prevention: Classify data by sensitivity; identify which data needs encryption at rest and in transit
Don't store sensitive data unnecessarily; discard it as soon as possible
Encrypt all data in transit with TLS 1.2+ and forward secrecy ciphers; enforce with HSTS
Use strong adaptive salted hashing for passwords: Argon2id, scrypt, bcrypt, or PBKDF2
Use authenticated encryption (GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305), not just encryption
Generate keys cryptographically randomly; store as byte arrays, not in source code
Disable caching for responses containing sensitive data
Avoid deprecated algorithms: MD5, SHA-1, DES, 3DES, RC4, PKCS#1 v1.5
Prepare for post-quantum cryptography by 2030 for high-risk systems

categories.name: Injection
categories.description: An application sends untrusted data to an interpreter as part of a command or query, causing unintended execution. Includes SQL, NoSQL, OS command, ORM, LDAP, Expression Language, and cross-site scripting (XSS). The application is vulnerable when user-supplied data is not validated, filtered, or sanitized.

key_cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Output

common_vulnerabilities: SQL queries built with string concatenation or f-strings containing user input
ORM methods used unsafely (Django .extra(), .raw(), HQL concatenation)
OS commands executed with user input via os.system(), subprocess with shell=True
eval()/exec() called on user-controlled data
Unsafe deserialization: pickle.loads(), yaml.load() without SafeLoader
Template injection: user input used in Jinja2/Mako template strings
LDAP queries built with string concatenation
XSS: user input rendered in HTML without escaping (dangerouslySetInnerHTML, innerHTML)
Header injection via unvalidated input in HTTP response headers
prevention: Use safe APIs that avoid the interpreter entirely: parameterized queries, ORMs
Positive server-side input validation (allow-lists, not deny-lists)
Escape special characters using interpreter-specific escape syntax for residual dynamic queries
Use LIMIT and other SQL controls to prevent mass data disclosure on injection
For OS commands: use subprocess.run() with a list of arguments and shell=False
For deserialization: use json.loads() instead of pickle; yaml.safe_load() instead of yaml.load()
For templates: never pass user input as the template string; pass as data to a fixed template
Use Content-Security-Policy (CSP) headers to mitigate XSS impact

categories.name: Insecure Design
categories.description: Missing or ineffective control design. Different from insecure implementation: a secure design can have implementation bugs (fixable), but an insecure design cannot be fixed by perfect implementation. Requires threat modeling, secure design patterns, and reference architectures.

key_cwes.name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type

common_vulnerabilities: No threat modeling performed for critical authentication, access control, or business logic flows
Trust boundary violations: untrusted data crosses into trusted context without validation
Missing rate limiting on sensitive operations (account creation, password reset, transactions)
Business logic flaws: no plausibility checks (e.g., bulk buying, negative quantities, price manipulation)
Unrestricted file upload without type/size validation or content scanning
Race conditions in multi-step transactions (TOCTOU)
Security questions for credential recovery (easily guessable or shared knowledge)
No anti-bot or anti-automation controls on high-value operations
Missing tenant isolation in multi-tenant architectures
prevention: Establish a Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC) with AppSec professionals
Use threat modeling for authentication, access control, business logic, and key flows
Build and maintain a library of secure design patterns ('paved road' components)
Integrate security language and controls into user stories
Write unit and integration tests that validate resistance to the threat model
Implement plausibility checks at every tier (client, server, database)
Segregate tenant data and access by design, not just configuration
Limit resource consumption per user or service (rate limiting, quotas)
categories.scanner_note: This is inherently an LLM category. Regex cannot detect missing controls or design flaws. The LLM evaluates whether the design itself is secure, not just the implementation.

categories.name: Authentication Failures
categories.description: When an attacker can trick a system into recognizing an invalid or incorrect user as legitimate. Covers credential stuffing, brute force, weak passwords, missing MFA, insecure session management, and improper credential storage.

key_cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation with Host Mismatch
Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

common_vulnerabilities: Permits automated attacks (credential stuffing, brute force, password spraying)
Allows default, weak, or well-known passwords (admin/admin, Password1)
Weak credential recovery using knowledge-based answers (security questions)
Passwords stored in plain text or with weak/unsalted hashing
Missing or ineffective multi-factor authentication
Session IDs exposed in URLs or not rotated after login
Sessions not properly invalidated on logout or after inactivity timeout
Identical error messages for login success/failure (enumeration) OR different messages (also enumeration)
No rate limiting or account lockout on failed authentication attempts
SSO without coordinated Single Logout leaving orphaned sessions
prevention: Implement multi-factor authentication to prevent credential stuffing and brute force
Never ship with default credentials; check against top 10,000 weak passwords and breached credential databases
Follow NIST 800-63b digital identity guidelines for password policy
Do not enforce forced password rotation unless breach is suspected
Use the same generic error message for all authentication outcomes (prevent enumeration)
Rate-limit failed login attempts; implement progressive delays or account lockout
Use server-side, secure session manager that generates high-entropy random session IDs
Session IDs must not be in the URL; must be invalidated after logout, idle timeout, and absolute timeout

categories.name: Software or Data Integrity Failures
categories.description: Code and infrastructure that does not protect against integrity violations. Includes insecure deserialization, use of untrusted plugins/libraries/CDNs, insecure CI/CD pipelines, and auto-update mechanisms without integrity verification.

key_cwes.name: Inclusion of Functionality from Untrusted Control Sphere
Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes (Mass Assignment)
Download of Code Without Integrity Check

common_vulnerabilities: Insecure deserialization: pickle, marshal, shelve, jsonpickle, dill, Java serialization, PHP unserialize
Plugins, libraries, or modules sourced from untrusted repositories or CDNs without integrity verification
CI/CD pipeline without integrity checks: unauthorized code changes can be deployed
Auto-update functionality downloading and applying updates without signature verification
CDN scripts loaded without Subresource Integrity (SRI) hashes
Mass assignment: object properties set from unvalidated request data (Object.fromEntries, **kwargs)
DNS or cookie forwarding to untrusted third-party services
Unsigned firmware or software packages accepted from unofficial sources
prevention: Use digital signatures or similar mechanisms to verify software/data origin and integrity
Consume libraries only from trusted repositories; consider hosting an internal vetted mirror
Use software composition analysis (SCA) tools to verify components are free of known vulnerabilities
Review code changes to minimize the chance of malicious code introduction
Ensure CI/CD pipeline has proper segregation, configuration, and access control
Never send unsigned or unencrypted serialized data to untrusted clients without integrity checks
Add Subresource Integrity (SRI) attributes to all CDN-loaded scripts and stylesheets

categories.name: Security Logging and Alerting Failures
categories.description: Inability to detect, escalate, and respond to active breaches due to insufficient logging, unclear log messages, unmonitored logs, missing alerting, and inappropriate data capture. Without proper logging, breaches go undetected for months or years.

key_cwes.name: Improper Output Neutralization for Logs (Log Injection)
Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

common_vulnerabilities: Login attempts, failed authentication, and access control failures not logged
High-value transactions not logged with audit trail
Warnings and errors generate no log messages or unclear ones
Logs not monitored for suspicious activity
Logs stored only locally with no backup or centralization
Alerting thresholds and response escalation missing or ineffective
Alert fatigue from excessive false positives
Sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII) written to log files
Log injection: unescaped user input in log messages enables log forging
No real-time detection of active attacks
Using print() instead of structured logging framework
prevention: Log all login, access control, and input validation failures with sufficient user context for forensic analysis
Log both success and failure of security controls throughout the application
Format logs for easy consumption by log management/SIEM solutions
Encode log data properly to prevent injection attacks
Use append-only audit trails with integrity controls to prevent log tampering
Roll back error transactions and fail closed (default-deny on error)
Establish monitoring dashboards with alerts for suspicious patterns
Deploy honeytokens as attack traps within databases and user accounts
Never log sensitive data: passwords, session tokens, PII, API keys
Follow NIST 800-61r2 or later for incident response planning

categories.name: Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions
categories.description: Applications fail to prevent, detect, and respond to unusual situations, causing crashes, data exposure, and security vulnerabilities. Encompasses inadequate input validation, delayed error handling, unexpected environmental states, and inconsistent exception management. Enables logic bugs, overflows, race conditions, and authentication/authorization bypasses.

key_cwes.name: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
Missing Default Case in Multiple Condition Expression

common_vulnerabilities: Bare except:pass silently swallowing all errors including security failures
Failing open: catching exceptions and returning success/allowing access on error
Stack traces and internal error details exposed to end users
Unchecked return values from security-critical operations
Resource exhaustion from exception handling that fails to release resources (file handles, connections)
Race conditions in multi-step transactions without proper rollback on error
Missing finally/cleanup blocks leaving resources in inconsistent state
No global exception handler for unhandled errors
Missing timeout on external requests causing cascading failures
Financial operations without transactional rollback allowing partial completion exploits
prevention: Catch errors at the point where they occur and handle them meaningfully — never silently
Fail closed: on any error in a security check, default to deny access
Roll back entire transactions on error; never recover mid-transaction
Implement global exception handlers for missed scenarios
Create error notifications, logging, and conditional alerts
Apply rate limiting, resource quotas, and throttling to prevent resource exhaustion
Set timeouts on all external requests and resource acquisitions
Use finally blocks or context managers to ensure cleanup on exception
Return generic error messages to users; log full details server-side
Conduct stress testing and penetration testing targeting error handling paths
